summary: Markets are navigating a cautiously mixed mood as geopolitical uncertainty surrounding the Iran conflict continues to dominate sentiment, even as Trump declared the war "terminated" while simultaneously expressing dissatisfaction with Iran's latest proposals, leaving investors uncertain about the true resolution of hostilities. The Iran conflict remains the dominant macro driver, with its cascading effects visible across energy supply chains, manufacturing input costs — which hit a four-year high in April — and global shipping routes, particularly around the Strait of Hormuz where the U.S. Treasury has warned shippers against paying tolls. On the stock-specific front, Atlassian surged 28% on strong cloud and data center earnings, Sandisk is positioning to capitalize on rising memory prices with positive read-throughs for broader tech holdings, and Jim Cramer flagged continued upside in Apple and Eli Lilly following their post-earnings strength, while Spirit Airlines faces existential pressure amid a potential government bailout and looming liquidation. The primary risk factors commanding investor attention are an oil price shock — with Reuters warning investors are running out of time to hedge — persistent inflation in manufacturing inputs, and unresolved Iran nuclear diplomacy that could rapidly reverse ceasefire conditions. In the short term, markets will remain headline-driven and volatile, with energy stocks and global supply chain-exposed sectors particularly sensitive to any deterioration in Iran ceasefire conditions, while tech earnings strength offers a partial counterweight to broader macro anxiety.
